text: The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum using a ligature device, creating an entry point for further dissection in the abdominal cavity.
The surgeon identifies the duodenum and uses a ligasure device to perform extensive Kocherization, mobilizing the duodenum to the level of the aorta.
The surgeon identifies the infrapancreatic superior mesenteric vein, which gives rise to the right gastroepiploic vein. The right gastroepiploic vein is double-clipped and divided.
The surgeon isolates the gastroduodenal artery branching from the common hepatic artery, clips it, and divides it using surgical instruments.
The portal vein and common bile duct are exposed, positioned laterally to the common hepatic artery, revealing their anatomical relationship within the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects and removes the lymph node chain along the proper hepatic artery, extending the dissection to the level of the celiac axis.
The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, applies two clips to secure it, and then divides the duct.

The surgeon isolates the common hepatic duct using umbilical tape and divides it above the insertion of the cystic duct. The distal end of the duct is controlled with a vicryl endo loop, while the proximal end is secured with a laparoscopic bulldog clamp.
The surgeon uses a linear laparoscopic stapler to cross staple the antrum of the stomach, completing the closure with two firings of the stapler.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative ultrasound to confirm that the mass is clear of adjacent vessels, visually assessing the proximity of the mass to surrounding vascular structures.
The surgeon creates a retropancreatic window and passes umbilical tape to guide the transection of the pancreatic neck, which is then accomplished using a ligature device.
The surgeon cross-staples the jejunum and mobilizes it, delivering the segment to the right subhepatic space.
The surgeon carefully divides the remaining attachments of the specimen to the superior mesenteric vein using a combination of ligature and hook cautery, followed by the removal of the specimen.
The surgeon completes the hepaticojejunostomy using 3-0 Vicryl sutures on an SH needle, connecting the hepatic duct to the jejunum.

The surgeon constructs the pancreaticojejunostomy in two layers using 3-0 silk sutures on an RB needle, employing the duct mucosa technique. The pancreatic duct is clearly visible as the anastomosis begins.
The surgeon constructs the gastro-jejunostomy, closing the common channel with two firings of the laparoscopic linear stapler, completing the anastomosis.
